text: BM25 is a bag-of-words ranking function that ranks documents by term frequency and inverse document frequency.
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into embedding vectors and retrieves by cosine similarity.
Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines several ranked lists by summing reciprocal ranks, fusing lexical and semantic retrievers.
Hybrid search blends lexical and dense retrieval so exact term matches and semantic matches reinforce one another.
A cross-encoder reranker rescores the top candidates by jointly reading the query and document for higher precision.
An embedding maps text into a vector space where semantically related passages sit close together.
Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain rewards placing relevant documents near the top of the ranking.
Recall at k measures the fraction of relevant documents retrieved within the top k results.
Mean Reciprocal Rank averages the reciprocal of the rank of the first relevant document.
A knowledge graph stores entities as nodes and relationships as edges, enabling multi-hop reasoning.
Multi-hop retrieval traverses graph relationships to gather evidence spread across several connected documents.
Chunking splits long documents into passages so retrieval can return focused, relevant spans.
A vector index such as HNSW enables approximate nearest neighbor search over millions of embeddings.
Hallucination is when a model generates fluent text that is unsupported by the retrieved evidence.
Faithfulness checks that every claim in a generated answer is grounded in the retrieved context.
The Model Context Protocol is an open standard that lets AI clients call external tools and data sources.
Tokenization breaks text into tokens; subword tokenization handles rare and morphological word variants.
Query expansion adds synonyms and related terms to a query to improve recall of relevant documents.
Offline retrieval evaluation relies on labeled query document relevance judgments called qrels.
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors while ignoring their magnitude.
An inverted index maps each term to the list of documents that contain it for fast lexical lookup.
Semantic search retrieves by meaning rather than exact keywords, matching paraphrases and synonyms.
